quiz.question: The therapist described her patient as other-directed — he shaped his opinions, clothes, and hobbies entirely to match whoever he was with. What does 'other-directed' mean?
options: He was naturally generous and prioritized others' needs
He was focused on external validation rather than his own values
He was skilled at reading and adapting to social situations, as the evidence showed
He was selfless and indifferent to personal gain
quiz.answer: He was focused on external validation rather than his own values
quiz.explanation:
Other-directed means guided by the expectations and opinions of others rather than one's own values.
• other-directed = conforming to external social pressures; lacking inner direction
• Term coined by sociologist David Riesman in 'The Lonely Crowd' (1950)
• Opposite: inner-directed = guided by internalized values

quiz.question: The children's undirected play turned out to be more creative than structured lessons. What does 'undirected' mean here?
options: The play was unobserved and therefore potentially unsafe
The play was free and without any guiding instructions
The play was quiet and individual rather than social
The play was outdoor rather than classroom-based
quiz.answer: The play was free and without any guiding instructions
quiz.explanation:
Undirected means not guided or controlled — without direction or instruction.
• undirected = having no specific aim or guidance imposed from outside
• 'Undirected energy', 'undirected research' — free-ranging and self-led
• Often productive precisely because it is unconstrained

quiz.question: The documentary showed a blighted industrial town where unemployment had reached 40%. What does 'blighted' mean here?
options: The town was isolated and difficult to reach
The town had been damaged and spoiled by adverse conditions
The town had been ignored by successive governments
The town had recently undergone significant demographic change
quiz.answer: The town had been damaged and spoiled by adverse conditions
quiz.explanation:
Blighted means damaged and deteriorated — having had growth or prosperity destroyed.
• blighted = damaged; having one's hopes or quality ruined
• From 'blight' — a disease or pest that destroys crops
• 'A blighted life', 'a blighted neighborhood' — potential destroyed by outside harm

quiz.question: He was visibly discomfited by the unexpected question about his expenses. What does 'discomfited' mean?
options: He was angry at being singled out in public
He was made to feel uneasy and embarrassed
He was confused and unsure how to interpret the question
He was insulted by the implication behind the question
quiz.answer: He was made to feel uneasy and embarrassed
quiz.explanation:
Discomfited means made uncomfortable and embarrassed — thrown off balance.
• discomfited = made uneasy; unsettled and embarrassed
• From Old French: 'desconfire' — to defeat, undo
• Note: not the same as 'discomfort' — discomfited implies social or psychological unease

quiz.question: The supervisor said the promotion was merited based on three years of outstanding work. What does 'merited' mean?
options: The promotion was approved by the board
The promotion was earned and deserved
The promotion was awarded earlier than usual
The promotion was the result of an internal review process
quiz.answer: The promotion was earned and deserved
quiz.explanation:
Merited means deserved — earned through one's actions or qualities.
• merited = justly deserved; warranted by merit
• From Latin: 'merere' — to earn, deserve
• 'Merited praise', 'merited punishment' — the outcome matches what was earned

quiz.question: He had suffered years of unrequited love. What does 'unrequited' mean?
options: His love was forbidden by his family and society
His love was not returned by the person he loved
His love had faded over time without being acted upon
His love was genuine but poorly expressed
quiz.answer: His love was not returned by the person he loved
quiz.explanation:
Unrequited means not returned or reciprocated.
• unrequited = not reciprocated; given without anything in return
• From Latin: 'requiter' — to pay back
• 'Unrequited love' = loving someone who does not love you back

quiz.question: The speech was stilted — every sentence sounded rehearsed and unnatural. What does 'stilted' describe?
options: The speech was very long and covered too many topics
The speech was stiff and unnaturally formal in style
The speech was delivered too quietly for the back of the room
The speech was poorly structured with no clear argument
quiz.answer: The speech was stiff and unnaturally formal in style
quiz.explanation:
Stilted means stiffly formal and unnatural — as if walking on stilts.
• stilted = unnaturally stiff; lacking ease or fluency
• From 'stilts' — raises one artificially above the natural level
• 'Stilted conversation', 'stilted prose' — formal in a wooden, awkward way

quiz.question: The board agreed that the CEO's dismissal was warranted. What does 'warranted' mean?
options: The dismissal had been approved by the legal team
The dismissal was justified and had adequate grounds
The dismissal had been anticipated by the CEO himself
The dismissal was accompanied by a financial settlement
quiz.answer: The dismissal was justified and had adequate grounds
quiz.explanation:
Warranted means justified — supported by sufficient reason or evidence.
• warranted = justified; having adequate grounds
• From 'warrant' — an authorization or guarantee
• Opposite: unwarranted (without justification)

quiz.question: The review concluded that the search had been unwarranted and violated the suspect's rights. What does 'unwarranted' mean?
options: The search had been conducted without the correct paperwork
The search lacked any justification or legal basis
The search had been carried out by officers without authority
The search had produced no relevant evidence
quiz.answer: The search lacked any justification or legal basis
quiz.explanation:
Unwarranted means without justification — having no adequate grounds.
• unwarranted = not justified; lacking sufficient reason
• 'Unwarranted interference', 'unwarranted assumption' — done without proper basis
• Opposite: warranted

quiz.question: She chose an unscented moisturizer for her sensitive skin. What does 'unscented' mean?
options: The product contained only natural ingredients
The product had no added fragrance
The product had been tested for allergic reactions
The product was suitable for very young children
quiz.answer: The product had no added fragrance
quiz.explanation:
Unscented means containing no added scent or fragrance.
• unscented = free of added fragrance
• 'Un-' (not) + 'scented' (having a scent added)
• Important for people with fragrance sensitivities — note: unscented ≠ odorless

quiz.question: Scientists described the glacier's rate of retreat as unprecedented. What does 'unprecedented' mean?
options: The rate was faster than scientists had predicted
The rate had never been observed or recorded before
The rate was alarming and required immediate action
The rate had been accelerating for the past two decades
quiz.answer: The rate had never been observed or recorded before
quiz.explanation:
Unprecedented means never having happened or existed before — without precedent.
• unprecedented = never before seen; without historical parallel
• From Latin: 'prae-' (before) + 'cedere' (to go) — nothing has gone before it
• 'Unprecedented scale', 'unprecedented decision' — entirely new territory

quiz.question: The old man's demented behavior — shouting at the walls, refusing to recognize his family — alarmed the carers. What does 'demented' describe here?
options: Behavior caused by extreme physical pain
Behavior caused by severe mental deterioration
Behavior resulting from grief and loss
Behavior triggered by a sudden traumatic event, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: Behavior caused by severe mental deterioration
quiz.explanation:
Demented means suffering from dementia — severe cognitive and mental deterioration.
• demented = afflicted with dementia; having lost normal mental functioning
• From Latin: 'de-' (away from) + 'mens' (mind)
• Also informal (hyperbolic): 'demented with worry' = nearly out of one's mind

quiz.question: The farmer lived a contented life — modest but with everything she needed. What does 'contented' mean?
options: She was wealthy enough to have no financial concerns, as the evidence showed
She was satisfied and at peace with her circumstances
She was too busy to dwell on what she lacked
She was optimistic about the future of her farm
quiz.answer: She was satisfied and at peace with her circumstances
quiz.explanation:
Contented means satisfied and at ease — at peace with one's situation.
• contented = feeling quiet satisfaction; not wanting more than one has
• From Latin: 'contentus' — held together, satisfied
• Quieter than 'happy' — contented is a settled, undramatic satisfaction

quiz.question: The detective said she was acquainted with the victim but not a close friend. What does 'acquainted' mean?
options: She had worked with the victim professionally in the past
She knew the victim slightly — familiar but not intimate
She had been formally introduced to the victim once
She recognized the victim but had never spoken to them
quiz.answer: She knew the victim slightly — familiar but not intimate
quiz.explanation:
Acquainted means having slight knowledge of someone — familiar but not close.
• acquainted = having some knowledge of a person or thing; not a stranger
• From Old French: 'acointier' — to make known
• 'Well-acquainted' = more familiar; 'getting acquainted' = beginning to know each other

quiz.question: She greeted the news with unwonted cheerfulness — she was normally quite reserved. What does 'unwonted' mean?
options: Her cheerfulness was clearly forced and unconvincing
Her cheerfulness was unusual and out of character
Her cheerfulness was inappropriate given the circumstances
Her cheerfulness surprised even her closest friends
quiz.answer: Her cheerfulness was unusual and out of character
quiz.explanation:
Unwonted means uncharacteristic — not what is usual or customary for that person.
• unwonted = not habitual or customary; unusual for that person
• From 'won't' (archaic for 'accustomed to'): 'un-wonted' = not accustomed
• Similar to 'unaccustomed': 'unwonted silence' = a silence not typical of that person

quiz.question: She was devoted to her students and often stayed late to help them. What does 'devoted' mean here?
options: She was professionally obligated to support her students
She was deeply committed and gave herself wholeheartedly
She was praised by her school for exceptional dedication
She was well-liked and respected by all her classes
quiz.answer: She was deeply committed and gave herself wholeheartedly
quiz.explanation:
Devoted means deeply committed and dedicated — giving oneself wholly to something.
• devoted = very loving and loyal; wholeheartedly committed
• From Latin: 'devovere' — to vow, dedicate
• 'Devoted parent', 'devoted follower' — the devotion is total and genuine

quiz.question: The team's halfhearted defense let in three goals in the second half. What does 'halfhearted' describe?
options: The defense was too aggressive and left gaps at the back
The defense was lacking in effort and commitment
The defense was disorganized due to a late substitution
The defense struggled against a much faster attack
quiz.answer: The defense was lacking in effort and commitment
quiz.explanation:
Halfhearted means without enthusiasm or effort — only partly committed.
• halfhearted = showing little interest or effort; unenthusiastic
• Only 'half' the heart is in it — the rest is elsewhere
• 'A halfhearted attempt', 'halfhearted applause' — going through the motions

quiz.question: The guide warned that the mountain route was not for the fainthearted. What was she implying?
options: Those with heart conditions should not attempt the climb
Those who lack courage and resolve should not attempt it
Those without proper equipment should turn back
Those unfamiliar with mountain terrain would struggle
quiz.answer: Those who lack courage and resolve should not attempt it
quiz.explanation:
Fainthearted means lacking courage — timid and easily discouraged.
• fainthearted = cowardly; not bold or resolute
• 'Faint' (weak) + 'heart' — the heart lacks the strength to carry on
• 'Not for the fainthearted' = demands courage and determination

quiz.question: The stouthearted crew refused to abandon ship even as the storm worsened. What does 'stouthearted' describe?
options: Their physical strength and endurance in the storm
Their brave and determined refusal to give up
Their loyalty to the captain above all else
Their experience handling ships in rough conditions
quiz.answer: Their brave and determined refusal to give up
quiz.explanation:
Stouthearted means brave and resolute — having a strong, courageous heart.
• stouthearted = bold, determined, and courageous
• 'Stout' here means strong and firm — not physically large
• Direct opposite of fainthearted

quiz.question: The soldier was court-martialled for deserting his post. What had he done?
options: He had disobeyed a direct order from his commanding officer
He had abandoned his post without permission
He had surrendered to the enemy without resistance
He had refused to carry out a mission he considered unethical
quiz.answer: He had abandoned his post without permission
quiz.explanation:
To desert means to abandon a duty or post without permission.
• deserted = abandoned; left without authorization
• From Latin: 'deserere' — to forsake, leave
• Military desertion = leaving one's post in wartime, a serious offense

quiz.question: The document was a purported copy of the original agreement. What does 'purported' tell us?
options: The document had been legally notarized
The document claimed to be something that may or may not be true
The document was a summary rather than a full copy
The document had been altered since the original was signed, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: The document claimed to be something that may or may not be true
quiz.explanation:
Purported means claimed or alleged — said to be something, but not verified.
• purported = reputed; supposed; claimed to be, without proof
• From Old French: 'purporter' — to convey, contain
• 'A purported expert', 'a purported confession' — the claim is unconfirmed

quiz.question: The gift shop sold assorted chocolates in every box. What does 'assorted' mean?
options: Each box contained chocolates of the highest quality
Each box contained a mixed variety of different kinds
Each box was priced according to the number of pieces, as the evidence showed
Each box was individually wrapped and sealed
quiz.answer: Each box contained a mixed variety of different kinds
quiz.explanation:
Assorted means consisting of various different kinds mixed together.
• assorted = made up of various sorts or varieties
• From 'sort' — divided into different types, then mixed
• 'Assorted biscuits', 'assorted sizes' — variety is the defining feature

quiz.question: The arbitrator was chosen precisely because she was disinterested in the outcome. Why was this quality important?
options: She had no personal stake in the matter and could judge fairly
She had no prior knowledge of the case and could approach it fresh
She found the case too technical to have a strong opinion on
She was known for giving brief, efficient rulings
quiz.answer: She had no personal stake in the matter and could judge fairly
quiz.explanation:
Disinterested means impartial — having no personal stake in the outcome.
• disinterested = unbiased; not personally invested in the result
• Often confused with 'uninterested' (bored) — disinterested means impartial, not indifferent
• A disinterested party can be trusted to judge fairly

quiz.question: The tightfisted landlord refused to repair the heating, despite three written requests. What does 'tightfisted' mean?
options: He was controlling and held all decision-making power
He was unwilling to spend money even when necessary
He was suspicious of tenants and slow to trust them
He was too busy managing other properties to respond quickly
quiz.answer: He was unwilling to spend money even when necessary
quiz.explanation:
Tightfisted means unwilling to spend — mean and miserly with money.
• tightfisted = reluctant to spend or give money; stingy
• The fist is 'tight' — clenched around money, refusing to release it
• Synonyms: miserly, penny-pinching, close-fisted

quiz.question: She was the most committed volunteer at the shelter — never missing a shift in three years. What does 'committed' describe here?
options: She had signed a formal contract with the shelter
She was deeply dedicated and loyal to the cause
She was the most experienced worker at the shelter
She was legally obligated to complete her volunteer hours
quiz.answer: She was deeply dedicated and loyal to the cause
quiz.explanation:
Committed means fully dedicated — having pledged oneself wholeheartedly.
• committed = dedicated; pledged to a course of action or a cause
• From Latin: 'committere' — to entrust, join
• 'A committed teacher', 'committed to justice' — no half-measures

quiz.question: Several uncommitted delegates could still swing the vote at the party conference. What does 'uncommitted' mean?
options: The delegates were absent and had sent proxies instead
The delegates had not yet decided which side they would support
The delegates were opposed to the leadership candidate
The delegates were from smaller regions with fewer votes, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: The delegates had not yet decided which side they would support
quiz.explanation:
Uncommitted means not yet pledged to any particular position or cause.
• uncommitted = not having declared or decided a position
• The opposite of committed — still open; a 'floating voter' in political terms
• 'Uncommitted delegates', 'uncommitted to a decision' — still undecided

quiz.question: The stowaway went unspotted for the entire voyage. What does 'unspotted' mean?
options: She hid in a place with no windows or natural light
She was not noticed or detected by anyone on board
She was helped by a crew member who kept her secret
She wore clothes that blended into the ship's interior
quiz.answer: She was not noticed or detected by anyone on board
quiz.explanation:
Unspotted means not seen or detected — having escaped notice.
• unspotted = not observed; going unnoticed
• 'Un-' (not) + 'spotted' (seen, detected)
• 'An unspotted error', 'going unspotted' — passed without being caught

quiz.question: The instructions were so convoluted that nobody could follow them. What does 'convoluted' mean?
options: The instructions were written in a foreign language, as the evidence showed
The instructions were excessively complex and hard to follow
The instructions were too vague to be useful
The instructions were missing several key steps
quiz.answer: The instructions were excessively complex and hard to follow
quiz.explanation:
Convoluted means intricately complex and difficult to understand — unnecessarily twisted.
• convoluted = excessively involved and difficult to follow
• From Latin: 'convolvere' — to roll together, entwine
• 'A convoluted argument', 'convoluted plot' — twisted back on itself

quiz.question: The response to the announcement was muted — a few polite claps, nothing more. What does 'muted' mean here?
options: The audience was confused by the announcement
The response was restrained and lacking in enthusiasm
The audience was too shocked to respond properly
The acoustics in the room made the applause hard to hear
quiz.answer: The response was restrained and lacking in enthusiasm
quiz.explanation:
Muted means restrained and subdued — lacking force or enthusiasm.
• muted = subdued; not strong, loud, or enthusiastic
• From 'mute' — silenced or softened
• 'Muted response', 'muted colors' — everything is turned down in intensity

quiz.question: The team was subdued at training the morning after their defeat. What does 'subdued' describe?
options: They were physically exhausted from the previous match
They were quiet, low in spirits, and lacking their usual energy
They were focused and concentrating on the next fixture, as the evidence showed
They were resentful of the coach's tactical decisions
quiz.answer: They were quiet, low in spirits, and lacking their usual energy
quiz.explanation:
Subdued means quiet and lacking in normal vitality — brought low.
• subdued = reduced in intensity; quiet and low in spirit
• From Latin: 'subducere' — to lead under, conquer
• 'A subdued atmosphere', 'subdued lighting' — always turned down, pressed down

quiz.question: The judge described the offender's conduct as depraved. What does 'depraved' mean?
options: The conduct was illegal under multiple statutes
The conduct showed moral corruption and wickedness
The conduct was reckless and dangerous to others
The conduct was premeditated and carefully planned, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: The conduct showed moral corruption and wickedness
quiz.explanation:
Depraved means morally corrupt and wicked — having very low moral standards.
• depraved = morally debased; given over to evil or corruption
• From Latin: 'depravare' — to distort, corrupt
• 'A depraved act', 'depraved indifference' — moral rot at the core

quiz.question: She was visibly relieved when the doctor confirmed the scan was clear. What does 'relieved' describe?
options: She had been confident of a good result all along
She felt the lifting of anxiety after the good news
She thanked the doctor and quickly left the office, as the evidence showed
She broke down in tears of joy at the news
quiz.answer: She felt the lifting of anxiety after the good news
quiz.explanation:
Relieved means freed from anxiety or distress — feeling the burden lifted.
• relieved = experiencing the removal of worry or tension
• From Latin: 'relevare' — to lift up, alleviate
• Relief = the noun; relieved = the feeling when the anxiety lifts

quiz.question: The film's ending felt contrived — the hero survived against all logic just to give the audience a happy ending. What does 'contrived' mean?
options: The ending was unpopular and disliked by critics
The ending was artificial and clearly manipulated rather than natural
The ending was rushed due to the film's excessive running time
The ending contradicted events that had been established earlier, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: The ending was artificial and clearly manipulated rather than natural
quiz.explanation:
Contrived means artificially produced — forced rather than arising naturally.
• contrived = deliberately manufactured; lacking authenticity
• From 'contrive' — to invent, plan
• 'Contrived dialogue', 'contrived plot twist' — you can see the hand of the author

quiz.question: He remained unmoved by her tears and refused to change his decision. What does 'unmoved' mean?
options: He was not physically in a position to respond to her
He was not emotionally affected and held firm
He had not yet been told the full details of the situation
He was indifferent to the consequences of his decision
quiz.answer: He was not emotionally affected and held firm
quiz.explanation:
Unmoved means not affected emotionally — remaining firm and unswayed.
• unmoved = not stirred by emotion; not persuaded or touched
• 'Un-' (not) + 'moved' (emotionally affected)
• 'Unmoved by pleas', 'unmoved by beauty' — either cold or simply resolute

quiz.question: The player received undeserved criticism after the team's loss — he had actually played well. What does 'undeserved' mean?
options: The criticism was anonymous and cowardly
The criticism was not merited by anything he had done
The criticism was exaggerated and disproportionate
The criticism came from people who had not watched the match
quiz.answer: The criticism was not merited by anything he had done
quiz.explanation:
Undeserved means not earned or warranted — given without just cause.
• undeserved = not merited; received without having done anything to earn it
• 'Undeserved praise' is just as undeserved as undeserved blame
• Opposite: merited, deserved

quiz.question: She gave the project her unreserved support. What does 'unreserved' mean here?
options: She had not been formally consulted before giving support
Her support was total and given without any hesitation or conditions
Her support was public and stated loudly in front of colleagues, as the evidence showed
Her support was unexpected given her previous position
quiz.answer: Her support was total and given without any hesitation or conditions
quiz.explanation:
Unreserved means complete and without qualification — holding nothing back.
• unreserved = total; not limited by any reservation or condition
• 'Unreserved apology', 'unreserved praise' — fully given, nothing held back
• Distinct from 'undeserved' — unreserved is about completeness, not merit

quiz.question: The players walked out onto the hallowed turf of the stadium. What does 'hallowed' mean here?
options: The turf was of exceptionally high technical quality
The ground was regarded with deep reverence and respect
The stadium had recently been renovated and restored
The pitch had hosted many historic international matches
quiz.answer: The ground was regarded with deep reverence and respect
quiz.explanation:
Hallowed means regarded as holy or sacred — treated with deep reverence.
• hallowed = honored and revered; made sacred by tradition or association
• From Old English: 'halgian' — to make holy
• 'Hallowed ground', 'hallowed institutions' — venerated through history and tradition

quiz.question: The editor cut the phrase 'at the end of the day' as it was too hackneyed. Why?
options: It was grammatically incorrect in formal writing
It had been used so often it had lost all meaning and freshness
It was too informal for the publication's house style
It was ambiguous and could be interpreted in multiple ways, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: It had been used so often it had lost all meaning and freshness
quiz.explanation:
Hackneyed means overused and therefore lacking originality — a worn-out cliché.
• hackneyed = made trite by overuse; unoriginal
• From 'hackney' — a horse for hire (overworked and worn out)
• 'A hackneyed phrase' was once vivid but has been used to death

quiz.question: The hospital set up a specialized unit for treating burns. What does 'specialized' mean here?
options: The unit was equipped with the most expensive technology
The unit was dedicated to one specific type of treatment
The unit was located in a separate building from the main hospital
The unit was only accessible to patients referred by a specialist
quiz.answer: The unit was dedicated to one specific type of treatment
quiz.explanation:
Specialized means focused on a specific area — not general but narrowly expert.
• specialized = adapted or limited to a particular function or skill
• From 'special' — apart from the general
• 'Specialized knowledge', 'specialized equipment' — tailored to one purpose

quiz.question: After thirty years in prison, the man had become institutionalized. What does this mean?
options: He had accepted his guilt and was seeking rehabilitation
He had become so dependent on institutional life he could not function outside it
He had taken on a leadership role within the prison community, as the evidence showed
He had been transferred between several different prisons
quiz.answer: He had become so dependent on institutional life he could not function outside it
quiz.explanation:
Institutionalized means having become so accustomed to an institution's routines that independent life feels impossible.
• institutionalized = adapted to institutional life to the point of dependency
• Also: 'institutionalized racism' = racism embedded into the structures of institutions
• The two senses share the idea of something becoming built into a system

quiz.question: The consultant said the company's workforce was underutilized — talented people doing basic tasks. What does 'underutilized' mean?
options: The workforce was too large for the company's current needs
The workforce was not being used to its full potential
The workforce was underpaid relative to industry standards
The workforce had received insufficient training and development
quiz.answer: The workforce was not being used to its full potential
quiz.explanation:
Underutilized means not used as fully or effectively as possible.
• underutilized = not exploited to its full capacity
• 'Under-' (below capacity) + 'utilized' (made use of)
• 'Underutilized talent', 'underutilized resources' — potential wasted through insufficient use

quiz.question: In the sailor's legend, a mermaid lured him onto the rocks. What is a mermaid?
options: A supernatural storm spirit of the open ocean
A mythical creature with a woman's upper body and a fish's tail
A type of sea creature known for mimicking human voices, as the evidence showed
A female sea goddess worshipped by ancient mariners
quiz.answer: A mythical creature with a woman's upper body and a fish's tail
quiz.explanation:
A mermaid is a mythical sea creature with the upper body of a woman and the tail of a fish.
• mermaid = from 'mere' (sea) + 'maid' (girl) — a girl of the sea
• From Old English: 'mere' — a body of water
• Mermaids appear in folklore worldwide as beautiful, sometimes dangerous, sea beings

quiz.question: Police conducted a raid on the warehouse at dawn. What did they do?
options: They sealed off all exits and waited for a warrant
They made a sudden surprise entry to search or arrest
They placed the warehouse under covert surveillance
They questioned the owners without entering the premises
quiz.answer: They made a sudden surprise entry to search or arrest
quiz.explanation:
A raid is a sudden surprise attack or entry — to search, seize, or arrest.
• raid = a sudden, swift incursion; a surprise attack or police operation
• From Old English: 'rad' — a riding, journey (originally a border attack on horseback)
• 'Dawn raid', 'police raid', 'air raid' — always sudden and without warning

quiz.question: The coach upbraided the striker for missing three easy chances. What did the coach do?
options: She praised the striker while noting areas for improvement
She scolded and reproached the striker sharply
She dropped the striker from the next match's lineup
She publicly defended the striker against media criticism
quiz.answer: She scolded and reproached the striker sharply
quiz.explanation:
To upbraid means to scold or reproach someone strongly.
• upbraid = to find fault with and rebuke; to scold sharply
• From Old English: 'upbredan' — to weave in, bring up as a reproach
• 'Upbraid' is more formal and severe than 'scold' — it implies strong moral reproach

quiz.question: The club's staid atmosphere put off younger members who wanted something livelier. What does 'staid' describe?
options: The atmosphere was exclusive and hard to break into
The atmosphere was settled and resistant to change or excitement
The atmosphere was formal and required specific dress codes
The atmosphere was dominated by older, more conservative members, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: The atmosphere was settled and resistant to change or excitement
quiz.explanation:
Staid means settled and unchanging — sedate and resistant to anything new.
• staid = respectable and unadventurous; boringly conventional
• From 'stayed' — something that has been held still
• 'A staid institution', 'staid routine' — solid, safe, and dull

quiz.question: The politician was harassed by a group of rabid supporters who would accept no criticism of her. What does 'rabid' mean here?
options: The supporters were highly organized and well-funded, as the evidence showed
The supporters were fanatical and irrationally extreme
The supporters were aggressive and prone to violence
The supporters refused to engage with opposing views
quiz.answer: The supporters were fanatical and irrationally extreme
quiz.explanation:
Rabid means extreme and fanatical — beyond reason in one's beliefs or behavior.
• rabid = having an extreme, unreasoning enthusiasm; fanatical
• From the disease rabies — which causes uncontrolled, frenzied behavior
• 'A rabid nationalist', 'rabid fan' — consumed by irrational intensity

quiz.question: The contractor underbid for the project and ended up losing money. What had she done?
options: She had quoted a price lower than the actual cost justified
She had submitted her bid after the deadline had passed
She had failed to include all the required project components
She had bid against herself by submitting two separate quotes
quiz.answer: She had quoted a price lower than the actual cost justified
quiz.explanation:
To underbid means to offer too low a price — bidding less than the true value or cost.
• underbid = to bid less than is warranted or required
• In contracting: underbidding wins the job but eats into profit
• In card games (e.g. bridge): bidding less than the hand deserves

quiz.question: The court forbade the defendant from contacting the witness. What did the court do?
options: It warned the defendant that contact would be monitored
It officially prohibited and banned the contact by order
It recommended that no contact take place during the trial
It required the witness to report any attempted contact
quiz.answer: It officially prohibited and banned the contact by order
quiz.explanation:
To forbid means to officially prohibit — to order that something must not happen.
• forbid = to command that something must not be done; to prohibit
• From Old English: 'forbeodan' — to command against
• Stronger than 'advise against' — to forbid is a direct prohibition

quiz.question: The child had a morbid fascination with death — always drawing skeletons and reading about disasters. What does 'morbid' mean?
options: The fascination was unusual and hard to explain in a child, as the evidence showed
The fascination was unhealthily focused on death and dark subjects
The fascination was deeply upsetting to those around her
The fascination suggested she had experienced early trauma
quiz.answer: The fascination was unhealthily focused on death and dark subjects
quiz.explanation:
Morbid means having an unhealthy interest in death, disease, or disturbing things.
• morbid = characterized by an excessive interest in gloomy or unpleasant topics
• From Latin: 'morbus' — disease
• Medical sense: 'morbid obesity', 'morbidity rate' — relating to disease and death

quiz.question: The placid lake reflected the mountains perfectly. What does 'placid' describe?
options: The lake was very deep and unusually clear
The lake was calm and undisturbed, with no ripples
The lake was surrounded by dense pine forest
The lake was famous for its remarkable scenery, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: The lake was calm and undisturbed, with no ripples
quiz.explanation:
Placid means calm and peaceful — not disturbed or agitated.
• placid = serenely calm; not easily disturbed
• From Latin: 'placidus' — calm, gentle
• 'A placid temperament' = even-tempered; 'placid waters' = still and undisturbed

quiz.question: The scientist described the substance as viscid as she struggled to pour it from the container. What does 'viscid' mean?
options: The substance was acidic and required careful handling
The substance was thick, sticky, and resistant to flow
The substance was transparent and hard to see clearly
The substance was highly reactive when exposed to air
quiz.answer: The substance was thick, sticky, and resistant to flow
quiz.explanation:
Viscid means thick and sticky — having a glutinous, adhesive quality.
• viscid = having a glutinous consistency; sticky and slow-flowing
• From Latin: 'viscum' — mistletoe (whose berries produce a sticky substance)
• Related: 'viscous' — similarly sticky and thick

quiz.question: The writer praised the essayist's pellucid prose. What was he praising?
options: Its elegant and ornate use of language
Its clarity and transparency — easy to understand perfectly
Its playful and witty approach to serious subjects
Its rich and densely packed use of reference and allusion, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: Its clarity and transparency — easy to understand perfectly
quiz.explanation:
Pellucid means transparently clear — easy to understand or see through.
• pellucid = translucently clear; expressed with complete clarity
• From Latin: 'pellucere' — to shine through
• 'Pellucid water', 'pellucid argument' — so clear nothing is hidden or obscured

quiz.question: She gave a candid interview, admitting she had made serious mistakes in her career. What does 'candid' mean?
options: She was guarded and chose her words with great care
She was open and honest, not hiding anything
She was regretful and visibly emotional throughout
She was diplomatic and avoided naming specific people
quiz.answer: She was open and honest, not hiding anything
quiz.explanation:
Candid means frank and truthful — not hiding or shading the truth.
• candid = honest and straightforward; not holding back
• From Latin: 'candidus' — white, bright, pure (honest and clear)
• 'Candid camera', 'candid opinion' — unposed, unguarded, unfiltered

quiz.question: The tabloid exposed the sordid details of the affair. What does 'sordid' mean?
options: The details were dramatic and completely unexpected
The details were morally distasteful and wretched
The details involved criminal behavior of a serious nature
The details were embarrassing but not morally significant
quiz.answer: The details were morally distasteful and wretched
quiz.explanation:
Sordid means involving ignoble actions — dirty, morally repugnant.
• sordid = involving base or dishonorable behavior; squalid and corrupt
• From Latin: 'sordidus' — dirty, vile
• 'Sordid details', 'sordid affair' — grubby in a moral as well as physical sense

quiz.question: The rigid enforcement of minor rules annoyed the staff. What does 'rigid' describe here?
options: The enforcement was arbitrary and inconsistently applied
The enforcement was inflexible and allowed no exceptions
The enforcement was carried out by an outside body
The enforcement was new and not yet clearly understood
quiz.answer: The enforcement was inflexible and allowed no exceptions
quiz.explanation:
Rigid means stiff and inflexible — not bending to circumstances.
• rigid = unable to bend; not adapting to conditions
• From Latin: 'rigidus' — stiff, hard
• 'Rigid rules', 'rigid thinking' — unyielding, unable to accommodate nuance

quiz.question: The frigid reception from her former colleagues made clear she was not welcome back. What does 'frigid' mean here?
options: The reception was hostile and openly aggressive
The reception was cold, distant, and unwelcoming
The reception was polite but clearly awkward
The reception was brief and ended earlier than expected
quiz.answer: The reception was cold, distant, and unwelcoming
quiz.explanation:
Frigid means intensely cold — literally or in manner.
• frigid = very cold; also: showing no warmth or emotion
• From Latin: 'frigidus' — cold
• 'Frigid temperature' = extremely cold; 'frigid manner' = emotionally cold and distant

quiz.question: The professor's turgid lecture was full of elaborate language and very few ideas. What does 'turgid' mean here?
options: The lecture was aggressively opinionated
The lecture was pompously inflated and hard to read
The lecture was disorganized and difficult to follow
The lecture was overly long but contained useful information
quiz.answer: The lecture was pompously inflated and hard to read
quiz.explanation:
Turgid means pompously inflated — swollen with self-importance and empty language.
• turgid = (of language) bombastic, inflated, hard to read
• From Latin: 'turgidus' — swollen, bloated
• 'Turgid prose' is puffed up with unnecessary verbosity; style overwhelms content

quiz.question: She grew orchids on her windowsill. What are orchids?
options: Edible root vegetables cultivated since ancient times, as the evidence showed
A family of often exotic and beautiful flowering plants
Climbing vines known for their large colorful leaves
Aquatic plants that grow in tropical rivers and ponds
quiz.answer: A family of often exotic and beautiful flowering plants
quiz.explanation:
An orchid is a member of a large family of flowering plants, many valued for their exotic beauty.
• orchid = a plant of the Orchidaceae family, known for spectacular flowers
• From Greek: 'orchis' — testicle (referring to the shape of the root tubers)
• One of the largest plant families; includes vanilla

quiz.question: The charity workers described the refugees' squalid living conditions. What does 'squalid' mean?
options: The conditions were dangerous due to nearby conflict
The conditions were dirty, overcrowded, and degrading
The conditions were poorly supplied with food and medicine
The conditions were temporary but rapidly deteriorating
quiz.answer: The conditions were dirty, overcrowded, and degrading
quiz.explanation:
Squalid means extremely dirty and unpleasant — degrading and wretched.
• squalid = dirty and miserable; showing neglect and poverty
• From Latin: 'squalere' — to be rough or dirty
• 'Squalid conditions', 'squalid flat' — not just untidy, but degradingly filthy

quiz.question: The stolid detective took notes without reacting as the witness broke down in tears. What does 'stolid' describe?
options: She was professionally trained to remain emotionally neutral
She was slow to react and showed little emotion or sensitivity
She was focused on the facts rather than the witness's distress
She was saving her reactions for the formal report
quiz.answer: She was slow to react and showed little emotion or sensitivity
quiz.explanation:
Stolid means calm and dependable but showing little emotion — impassive and dull.
• stolid = not easily moved or excited; impassive and unemotional
• From Latin: 'stolidus' — dull, slow-witted
• 'A stolid temperament' — reliable but unresponsive; steady but unimaginative

quiz.question: The greenhouse was kept humid for the tropical plants. What does 'humid' mean?
options: The greenhouse was kept at a consistently warm temperature
The air was kept moist and full of water vapor
The greenhouse was sealed to prevent temperature fluctuations
The plants were watered twice daily to maintain their health
quiz.answer: The air was kept moist and full of water vapor
quiz.explanation:
Humid means having a high level of moisture in the air — damp and muggy.
• humid = having a lot of moisture in the air; damp
• From Latin: 'humidus' — moist
• 'Humid climate', 'humid air' — the air feels wet and heavy

quiz.question: The doctor noted that the tissue around the injury was tumid. What did she observe?
options: The tissue had become discolored from bruising
The tissue was swollen and abnormally enlarged
The tissue had hardened and become stiff to the touch
The tissue had become tender and painful on contact
quiz.answer: The tissue was swollen and abnormally enlarged
quiz.explanation:
Tumid means swollen — abnormally enlarged.
• tumid = swollen; puffed up
• From Latin: 'tumere' — to swell
• Medically: tumid tissue is inflamed and swollen; figuratively: tumid prose is pompous

quiz.question: Spiders are arachnids, not insects. What distinguishes an arachnid from an insect?
options: Arachnids have wings and insects do not
Arachnids have eight legs while insects have six
Arachnids are always venomous and insects are not
Arachnids have an internal skeleton while insects have an external one
quiz.answer: Arachnids have eight legs while insects have six
quiz.explanation:
An arachnid is a class of arthropod characterized by eight legs and two body segments.
• arachnid = a member of the class Arachnida: spiders, scorpions, mites, ticks
• From Greek: 'Arachne' — a weaver turned into a spider in mythology
• Key distinction: 8 legs (arachnid) vs. 6 legs (insect)

quiz.question: Chimpanzees and gorillas are described as anthropoid apes. What does 'anthropoid' mean?
options: They are found only in regions where humans also live
They are apes that closely resemble humans in form
They are apes that have been studied by anthropologists
They are apes capable of using basic tools and language
quiz.answer: They are apes that closely resemble humans in form
quiz.explanation:
Anthropoid means resembling a human — human-like in form.
• anthropoid = resembling a human being in form or behavior
• From Greek: 'anthropos' (human being) + '-oid' (resembling)
• Anthropoid apes (chimps, gorillas, orangutans) are our closest living relatives

quiz.question: NASA tracked an asteroid passing close to Earth. What is an asteroid?
options: A small, rocky body orbiting the Sun
A fragment of a comet that has broken apart
A large cloud of gas and dust in deep space
A moon of one of the outer planets
quiz.answer: A small, rocky body orbiting the Sun
quiz.explanation:
An asteroid is a small rocky body that orbits the Sun, mostly found in the asteroid belt between Mars and Jupiter.
• asteroid = a rocky body in the solar system, smaller than a planet
• From Greek: 'aster' (star) + '-oid' (resembling) — star-like in appearance
• Distinct from comets (icy) and meteoroids (smaller fragments)

quiz.question: The surgeon performed the complex operation with remarkable sangfroid. What does 'sangfroid' mean?
options: A high level of technical precision and skill
Coolness and composure under pressure
Absolute focus and disregard for distractions
Confidence born of many years of experience
quiz.answer: Coolness and composure under pressure
quiz.explanation:
Sangfroid means cool composure in difficult situations — cold-blooded calm.
• sangfroid = calmness in a crisis; unruffled self-possession
• From French: 'sang' (blood) + 'froid' (cold) — cold blood
• 'Acting with sangfroid' = remaining composed when others would panic

quiz.question: The intrepid explorer crossed the Antarctic alone in the depths of winter. What does 'intrepid' describe?
options: Supremely fit and physically capable for the challenge
Fearless and resolutely brave in the face of danger
Highly experienced in surviving extreme conditions
Determined to prove a point to doubters and critics
quiz.answer: Fearless and resolutely brave in the face of danger
quiz.explanation:
Intrepid means fearlessly bold — undaunted by danger or difficulty.
• intrepid = resolutely courageous; not intimidated by danger
• From Latin: 'in-' (not) + 'trepidus' (alarmed, trembling)
• 'An intrepid reporter', 'intrepid adventurer' — going boldly where others fear to go

quiz.question: The response to the CEO's speech was tepid — polite but without enthusiasm. What does 'tepid' mean here?
options: The response was respectful and formally appropriate
The response was lukewarm — neither enthusiastic nor hostile
The response was divided between supporters and critics
The response was restrained due to the seriousness of the occasion
quiz.answer: The response was lukewarm — neither enthusiastic nor hostile
quiz.explanation:
Tepid means lukewarm — neither hot nor cold; lacking enthusiasm.
• tepid = only slightly warm; showing little enthusiasm
• From Latin: 'tepidus' — lukewarm
• 'Tepid response', 'tepid support' — just enough warmth not to be cold

quiz.question: The food critic described the pasta as insipid. What was wrong with it?
options: It was overcooked and unpleasantly mushy
It had no flavor — dull and completely lacking in taste
It was too heavily seasoned and overwhelmed the palate, as the evidence showed
It was presented sloppily and looked unappetizing
quiz.answer: It had no flavor — dull and completely lacking in taste
quiz.explanation:
Insipid means lacking flavor or interest — utterly dull and tasteless.
• insipid = without flavor; lacking in spirit or interest
• From Latin: 'in-' (not) + 'sapidus' (tasty, flavorful)
• 'Insipid food', 'insipid conversation' — so bland it offers nothing

quiz.question: The poet praised the limpid quality of the mountain stream. What does 'limpid' describe?
options: The stream was fast-moving and full of energy
The water was perfectly clear and transparent
The stream ran silently over smooth, flat stones
The water was cold enough to preserve fish and wildlife
quiz.answer: The water was perfectly clear and transparent
quiz.explanation:
Limpid means clear and transparent — perfectly easy to see through.
• limpid = pellucidly clear; unclouded
• From Latin: 'limpidus' — clear
• 'Limpid water', 'limpid eyes', 'limpid prose' — clarity and purity of the highest order

quiz.question: The expedition crossed an arid plateau with no water source for three days. What does 'arid' mean?
options: The plateau was at very high altitude
The plateau had extremely low rainfall and no moisture
The plateau was flat and featureless for miles in every direction
The plateau was dangerously hot during the daytime
quiz.answer: The plateau had extremely low rainfall and no moisture
quiz.explanation:
Arid means extremely dry — having very little or no rain.
• arid = having insufficient rainfall to support most plant life
• From Latin: 'aridus' — dry, parched
• 'Arid landscape', 'arid argument' — the figurative sense means dry and lifeless

quiz.question: The car was a hybrid — it used both a petrol engine and an electric motor. What is a hybrid?
options: A vehicle that runs purely on renewable energy
Something combining two different elements or systems
A vehicle that is more fuel-efficient than a standard model
Something that has been redesigned after an earlier failed version
quiz.answer: Something combining two different elements or systems
quiz.explanation:
A hybrid is something made by combining two different elements — a mixture of two things.
• hybrid = produced from two different species, systems, or sources
• From Latin: 'hybrida' — offspring of a tame sow and wild boar
• 'Hybrid car', 'hybrid approach', 'hybrid species' — always a combination of two

quiz.question: The tenor's florid vocal style featured elaborate runs and decorations on every phrase. What does 'florid' mean here?
options: His voice was exceptionally powerful and loud
His style was elaborately decorated and ornate
His delivery was unusually emotional and expressive
His technique was considered old-fashioned by modern standards
quiz.answer: His style was elaborately decorated and ornate
quiz.explanation:
Florid means elaborately ornate — overly decorated, as if covered in flowers.
• florid = excessively elaborate and ornate; overly flowery
• From Latin: 'floridus' — flowery, blooming
• 'Florid prose', 'florid complexion' (red and flushed), 'florid singing' — all mean rich excess

quiz.question: The documentary showed life in the torrid heat of the Sahara. What does 'torrid' mean here?
options: The heat was unpredictable and varied dramatically by day
The heat was parching and intensely burning
The heat was made worse by high levels of humidity
The heat lasted all year with no cooler season
quiz.answer: The heat was parching and intensely burning
quiz.explanation:
Torrid means very hot and dry — intensely scorching.
• torrid = very hot and dry; parching
• From Latin: 'torrere' — to parch, scorch
• 'Torrid zone' = the tropics; 'a torrid affair' = passionately intense

quiz.question: The investigators were hit by the fetid smell when they opened the container. What does 'fetid' mean?
options: The smell was strong and impossible to ignore
The smell was extremely unpleasant and rotten
The smell indicated a chemical substance was present
The smell was unfamiliar and difficult to identify
quiz.answer: The smell was extremely unpleasant and rotten
quiz.explanation:
Fetid means smelling extremely unpleasant — putrid and rotting.
• fetid = having a strong, offensive smell; stinking
• From Latin: 'fetere' — to stink
• 'Fetid water', 'fetid air' — the worst kind of bad smell; suggests rot and decay

quiz.question: She lay languid in the hammock, barely lifting a hand as her guests arrived. What does 'languid' describe?
options: She was deeply tired after a long and stressful week, as the evidence showed
She was pleasantly relaxed and moving with unhurried ease
She was too unwell to stand and greet her guests
She was making a deliberate show of not caring
quiz.answer: She was pleasantly relaxed and moving with unhurried ease
quiz.explanation:
Languid means pleasantly relaxed and slow-moving — pleasantly lacking in energy.
• languid = weak and relaxed; lacking vigor or energy
• From Latin: 'languidus' — faint, weak
• 'Languid afternoons', 'languid gesture' — unhurried, almost dreamy

quiz.question: The soprano's liquid voice flowed effortlessly through the concert hall. What does 'liquid' describe here?
options: Her voice was exceptionally powerful and resonant
Her voice was smooth, clear, and flowing — like water
Her voice was unusually high and brilliant in tone
Her voice had a quality that was difficult to define, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: Her voice was smooth, clear, and flowing — like water
quiz.explanation:
Liquid (figuratively) means smooth, clear, and flowing — like a pure liquid.
• liquid = (of sound or movement) flowing and clear
• From Latin: 'liquidus' — fluid, clear
• 'Liquid notes', 'liquid movement' — smooth and free-flowing

quiz.question: He was a fervid supporter of electoral reform, writing dozens of articles and attending every rally. What does 'fervid' mean?
options: He was a long-standing and publicly recognized advocate
He was intensely passionate and ardent in his support
He was well-informed and persuasive in his arguments
He was combative and prone to disagreeing with opponents
quiz.answer: He was intensely passionate and ardent in his support
quiz.explanation:
Fervid means intensely enthusiastic — burning with passion.
• fervid = intensely enthusiastic; ardently passionate
• From Latin: 'fervidus' — glowing, burning
• Stronger than 'keen' — fervid suggests heat and urgency in the feeling

quiz.question: His perfervid speech alarmed even his allies — the intensity seemed to go beyond reason. What does 'perfervid' mean?
options: The speech was impeccably prepared and flawlessly delivered
The speech was excessively and intensely impassioned
The speech was provocative and designed to cause controversy
The speech was long, repetitive, and hard to follow
quiz.answer: The speech was excessively and intensely impassioned
quiz.explanation:
Perfervid means intensely and excessively enthusiastic — even more extreme than fervid.
• perfervid = excessively ardent; impassioned beyond measure
• From Latin: 'per-' (through, thoroughly) + 'fervidus' (burning)
• 'Perfervid' is fervid turned all the way up — uncomfortably intense

quiz.question: The piebald horse stood out immediately in the field. What did it look like?
options: A uniform chestnut brown with a white blaze on the face
Having irregular patches of black and white
Pale grey all over with a darker mane and tail
A deep bay with four white stockings
quiz.answer: Having irregular patches of black and white
quiz.explanation:
Piebald means having patches of black and white — irregularly colored.
• piebald = (of a horse) having irregular patches of black and white
• From 'pie' (magpie — black and white) + 'bald' (white patch)
• Related: 'skewbald' = patches of brown and white; 'pied' = any two-color patching

quiz.question: The ribald humor of the comedian made some in the audience uncomfortable. What kind of humor was it?
options: Cruel and designed to humiliate specific people
Coarsely irreverent and dealing with sex in a crude way
Dark and focused on death and suffering
Politically provocative and likely to cause controversy, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: Coarsely irreverent and dealing with sex in a crude way
quiz.explanation:
Ribald means using vulgar humor about sexual subjects — coarsely irreverent.
• ribald = referring to sexual matters in an amusingly coarse way
• From Old French: 'ribaud' — rogue, licentious person
• 'Ribald jokes', 'ribald comedy' — earthy and cheerfully crude

quiz.question: The lawyers shielded their client from the press after the verdict. What did the lawyers do?
options: They released a prepared statement on the client's behalf
They protected the client from unwanted contact and scrutiny
They advised the client not to speak to the media
They escorted the client through a back exit to avoid cameras
quiz.answer: They protected the client from unwanted contact and scrutiny
quiz.explanation:
To shield means to protect from harm or unwanted attention — to act as a barrier.
• shield = to protect; to act as a defensive cover
• From Old English: 'scield' — a protective board held in battle
• 'Shield from criticism', 'shield from scrutiny' — providing cover or protection

quiz.question: The senator wielded enormous influence over the committee. What does 'wield' mean?
options: She had been given influence by a superior
She exercised and made active use of her influence
She was widely recognized for her influence
She was the most senior member of the committee, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: She exercised and made active use of her influence
quiz.explanation:
To wield means to hold and exercise — to use something as a tool of power.
• wield = to hold and use (a weapon, tool, or power) effectively
• From Old English: 'wieldan' — to govern, control
• 'Wield power', 'wield a sword' — active, purposeful exercise of something

quiz.question: The two orchestras' styles melded perfectly in the joint concert. What does 'meld' mean?
options: Each orchestra kept its own distinct style throughout
They blended together to form a seamless unified whole
They took turns performing to showcase their differences
The styles clashed but created an interesting tension
quiz.answer: They blended together to form a seamless unified whole
quiz.explanation:
To meld means to blend or merge — to combine into a unified whole.
• meld = to blend, merge, or unite harmoniously
• Possibly from 'melt' + 'weld' — a fusion of two things
• 'Meld ideas', 'meld two traditions' — the seam disappears in the joining

quiz.question: The mechanic welded the cracked frame back together. What did she do?
options: She sealed the crack with a strong adhesive compound
She joined the metal by melting and fusing the edges together
She reinforced the frame by bolting metal plates over the crack
She replaced the damaged section with a new piece of metal
quiz.answer: She joined the metal by melting and fusing the edges together
quiz.explanation:
To weld means to join metal by melting and fusing the pieces together.
• weld = to join materials by heating to melting point and pressing together
• From Old English/German: related to 'well' (to boil)
• Also figurative: 'welded by shared experience' = joined inseparably

quiz.question: Faced with mounting losses, the small restaurant finally folded. What happened?
options: The restaurant changed its menu and concept entirely
The restaurant closed down and ceased trading
The restaurant was sold to a larger chain
The restaurant reduced its opening hours to survive
quiz.answer: The restaurant closed down and ceased trading
quiz.explanation:
To fold means to cease operation — to shut down, especially a business.
• fold = (of a business) to collapse and close down
• From the image of closing or collapsing inward
• Also: 'fold' in card games = to give up; both senses involve giving in

quiz.question: The builders erected a scaffold around the entire building. What is a scaffold?
options: A temporary platform used for supporting workers during construction
A permanent metal framework within the walls of a building, as the evidence showed
A crane system attached to the top of a tall structure
A protective netting system to catch falling debris
quiz.answer: A temporary platform used for supporting workers during construction
quiz.explanation:
A scaffold is a temporary framework supporting workers and materials during construction or repair.
• scaffold = a temporary structure of poles and boards for builders to stand on
• Also: a scaffold was historically a raised platform for public executions
• From Old French: 'escafaut' — a platform

quiz.question: The project's manifold challenges included funding, logistics, and staff recruitment. What does 'manifold' mean?
options: The challenges were connected and influenced one another
The challenges were numerous and of many different kinds
The challenges were severe and potentially insurmountable
The challenges were unexpected and had not been planned for
quiz.answer: The challenges were numerous and of many different kinds
quiz.explanation:
Manifold means many and varied — of many kinds.
• manifold = many and various; of diverse kinds
• From Old English: 'manigfeald' — many-folded, diverse
• 'Manifold benefits', 'manifold problems' — emphasizes variety as well as quantity

quiz.question: The traveler reached the summit and beheld the entire valley stretched below. What does 'behold' mean?
options: She climbed further to get an even better vantage point
She saw and took in the scene before her
She photographed the view to show others later
She paused to rest before beginning the descent
quiz.answer: She saw and took in the scene before her
quiz.explanation:
To behold means to see — especially with wonder; to look at and observe.
• behold = to see; to observe with attention (often with awe)
• From Old English: 'behealdan' — to hold in view, observe
• 'Lo and behold' = look and see; 'behold a wonder' = gaze upon something remarkable

quiz.question: The company was fined for withholding information from the regulator. What does 'withhold' mean?
options: Submitting information in an incorrect format
Deliberately refusing to give or reveal something
Accidentally omitting key details from a submission
Delaying the submission beyond the agreed deadline
quiz.answer: Deliberately refusing to give or reveal something
quiz.explanation:
To withhold means to refuse to give or disclose — to keep back deliberately.
• withhold = to hold back; to refuse to give or reveal
• From Old English: 'with-' (against) + 'hold'
• 'Withhold evidence', 'withhold payment' — actively keeping something back

quiz.question: The startup had gained a foothold in the European market. What does 'foothold' mean here?
options: It had established a small but secure position from which to grow
It had been legally registered in a European jurisdiction
It had formed partnerships with two European distributors
It had attracted its first investment from a European firm, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: It had established a small but secure position from which to grow
quiz.explanation:
A foothold is a first secure position from which further advance becomes possible.
• foothold = a secure initial position; a base from which to progress
• From rock-climbing: a small ledge for the foot to stand on
• 'Gain a foothold' = establish the first beachhead before expanding

quiz.question: The politician was cast in the traditional mold of her party's leadership. What does 'mold' mean here?
options: She was trained and mentored by her party's senior figures
She conformed to an established pattern or type
She was considered too conventional to be truly effective
She had been carefully selected from among many candidates
quiz.answer: She conformed to an established pattern or type
quiz.explanation:
A mold (figuratively) is an established pattern, type, or character.
• mold = a distinctive character or type; a fixed pattern of formation
• From the tool used to shape liquid materials into a fixed form
• 'Cast in the same mold', 'break the mold' — conforming to or departing from a type

quiz.question: Customs officers seized a large quantity of contraband at the port. What is contraband?
options: Goods that are damaged and no longer fit for sale
Goods that are imported or exported illegally
Goods that are taxed at an unusually high rate
Goods that are subject to special safety inspections
quiz.answer: Goods that are imported or exported illegally
quiz.explanation:
Contraband is goods that are illegally imported or exported — smuggled items.
• contraband = smuggled goods; items prohibited by law from being traded
• From Italian: 'contrabbando' — against the ban
• 'Contraband cigarettes', 'contraband weapons' — illegally trafficked

quiz.question: After the treaty was signed, the militia was ordered to disband. What does 'disband' mean?
options: The militia was required to surrender its weapons
The militia was formally dissolved and ceased to exist as a group
The militia was placed under command of the national army
The militia was prohibited from operating in certain areas, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: The militia was formally dissolved and ceased to exist as a group
quiz.explanation:
To disband means to break up a group — to formally dissolve an organization.
• disband = to break up and cease to function as a group
• From 'dis-' (apart) + 'band' (a group)
• 'The band disbanded', 'the army disbanded' — the members go their separate ways

quiz.question: The food critic dismissed the restaurant's cooking as bland. What does 'bland' mean?
options: The cooking was technically competent but uninspired
The cooking was lacking in flavor or any distinctive character
The cooking was too simple for the restaurant's price point, as the evidence showed
The cooking was safe but unlikely to offend any palate
quiz.answer: The cooking was lacking in flavor or any distinctive character
quiz.explanation:
Bland means lacking strong flavor, character, or interest — mild to the point of dullness.
• bland = not stimulating; lacking any distinctive taste or character
• From Latin: 'blandus' — smooth, mild
• 'Bland food', 'bland personality' — inoffensive but forgettable

quiz.question: The village was decorated with garlands for the harvest festival. What are garlands?
options: Painted wooden signs hung outside buildings, as the evidence showed
Wreaths or chains of flowers, leaves, or other decorative items
Colored flags strung between buildings
Lanterns placed along the main street
quiz.answer: Wreaths or chains of flowers, leaves, or other decorative items
quiz.explanation:
A garland is a wreath or festive chain of flowers, leaves, or other decorative objects.
• garland = a decorative chain or wreath of flowers and foliage
• From Old French: 'garlande'
• Used as decoration on occasions of celebration — hung, worn, or draped

quiz.question: The coastal city relied on trade from its hinterland. What is the hinterland?
options: The trading partners across the sea
The inland area behind the coast that a city serves
The network of ports and harbors along the coastline
The fishing grounds located offshore
quiz.answer: The inland area behind the coast that a city serves
quiz.explanation:
The hinterland is the remote inland area beyond a coastal city or port — its backland.
• hinterland = the region behind a coast or river bank; a city's interior catchment area
• From German: 'hinter' (behind) + 'Land' (land)
• Also figurative: 'intellectual hinterland' = depth of knowledge behind someone's public face

quiz.question: The workers' union issued a demand for a 10% pay rise. What is a demand in this context?
options: A polite request made through the proper channels
A firm, insistent claim that something must be done
A formal complaint submitted to management
A suggestion offered as a starting point for negotiation
quiz.answer: A firm, insistent claim that something must be done
quiz.explanation:
A demand is a firm, insistent request — not a suggestion or a plea, but an assertion of entitlement.
• demand = an insistent and authoritative claim or requirement
• From Latin: 'demandare' — to entrust, order
• 'Supply and demand', 'in high demand' — the noun also means the level of desire for something

quiz.question: The officer was formally reprimanded for his conduct. What happened to him?
options: He was suspended without pay pending an investigation, as the evidence showed
He received an official rebuke and expression of disapproval
He was demoted to a lower rank
He was required to undergo retraining
quiz.answer: He received an official rebuke and expression of disapproval
quiz.explanation:
A reprimand is a formal rebuke — an official expression of strong disapproval.
• reprimand = a formal, official rebuke
• From French: 'réprimande' — from Latin 'reprimere' (to press back, restrain)
• Stronger than a warning; usually on record but less severe than suspension

quiz.question: The general countermanded the order to advance as new intelligence came in. What did she do?
options: She gave additional instructions alongside the original order, as the evidence showed
She cancelled the previous order and replaced it with a new one
She delayed the order until conditions improved
She passed the decision to a more senior officer
quiz.answer: She cancelled the previous order and replaced it with a new one
quiz.explanation:
To countermand means to cancel a previously issued order by issuing a contrary one.
• countermand = to revoke an order by issuing a contrary command
• From Latin: 'contra' (against) + 'mandare' (to order)
• 'Countermand an order' = the new order overrides and nullifies the old one
